coredata.dc:title: Challenges and Opportunities in Composite Cathode Production of Polymer-Based Solid-State Batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Dhom, Jonas
Röß-Ohlenroth, Richard
Stumper, Benedikt
Berger, Christoph
Daub, Rüdiger
coredata.dc:description: Higher energy densities and a high level of safety are decisive for the energy storage systems of electric vehicles to satisfy the high range requirements. The polymer-based solid-state battery is a promising technology that stands out due to its high energy density and theoretical safety advantages. Research into this innovative battery technology in Germany and Europe mainly focuses on the laboratory scale. For the production of polymer-based solid-state batteries, there is a lack of accessible information to transfer the laboratory scale to pilot production. Therefore, competence in upscaling the polymer-based composite cathode is essential. The current work investigates the cause-effect relationships of individual production processes of the polymer-based composite cathode in order to establish an in-depth understanding of how they can be scaled. In particular, mixing, coating, and calendering are compared with the corresponding processes of the conventional electrode production of lithium-ion battery cells to identify similarities and differences. The comparison demonstrates that the calendering process is particularly challenging due to the minimization of the porosity of the polymer-based composite cathode. For this reason, the challenges of calendering are discussed in detail. An existing empirical model of the calendering of lithium metal is used to enhance the comprehension of the calendering process. This results in a reduced material consumption and experimental setup time. By using a temporary foil to protect the calender rollers from the high adhesion, polymer-based composite cathodes can be calendered with a conventional roll-to-roll process. The results provide first informations of the scalability of the process of polymer-based composite cathodes from laboratory-based work to a larger scale.
coredata.dcterms:subject: solid-state battery
composite cathode
calendering
porosity
process
empirical modeling
concept

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Bayerische Staatsministerium für Wirtschaft, Landesentwicklung und Energie

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The presented work is part of the research project \u2018DiBatma-pro\u2019 (Digitalisierung in der Batteriematerial-und Batteriezellenproduktion). We express our gratitude to the Federal Ministry of Education and Research, the Bavarian Ministry of Economic Affairs, Regional Development and Energy, and the European Regional Development Fund for the project. Furthermore, we would like to thank our research partners for their constructive work and support with the DiBatma-pro project. Moreover, the authors are grateful for the support of Julian Wendel in the context of his student work and activities.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.com Procedia CIRP 130 (2024) 443â€“448 2212-8271 Â© 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 57th CIRP Conference on Manufacturing Systems 2024 (CMS 2024) 10.1016/j.procir.2024.10.112 Keywords: solid-state battery; composite cathode; calendering; porosity; process; empirical modeling; concept; 1. Indroduction The established lithium-ion battery (LIB) has enabled the electrification of vehicles [1]. However, the performance of the LIB is reaching physical limits [2], therefore it is essential to look into new battery types to push forward the future battery technology of vehicles. One promising next-generation battery technology is the solid-state battery (SSB), which is expected to have a significantly higher predicted energy density when compared to LIB [1, 3]. One reason for this is the use of a lithium metal anode, which has a high theoretical capacity of 3860 mAÂ·hÂ·g-1 [4]. In addition, SSB is characterized by high safety compared to LIB due to the absence of flammable liq- uid electrolyte [5, 6]. Most research into this innovative battery technology in Germany and Europe is focussed on the labora- tory scale. For polymer-based SSB, there is no accessible exper- tise for production technology on a larger scale with the stan- dard polymer polyethylene oxide (PEO). This is also required for the increasingly relevant hybrid SSB. In addition, there is no strategy for upscaling new polymer systems for the SSB. To bridge this information gap and utilize the advantages of SSB, the first part is to transfer laboratory scale production to pilot scale production. This requires an in-depth understanding of the process expertise in the individual processes. Polymer- based composite cathode (CC) has particularly high require- ments and possibilities. Therefore, the fundamental properties and processes of SSB are presented in the first step of this paper. The process steps illustrate, that calendering of polymer-based CC poses a particular challenge. Hence, the calendering process is explicitly described in detail. Based on this, a description is given on how the calendering of polymer-based CC can be re- alized on a pilot scale. Challenges and Opportunities in Composite Cathode Production of Polymer-Based Solid-State Batteries Jonas Dhoma*, Richard RoÌˆÃŸ-Ohlenrotha, Benedikt Stumpera, Christoph Bergera, RuÌˆdiger Dauba,b aFraunhofer Institute for Casting, Composite and Processing Technology IGCV, Am Technologiezentrum 10, 86159 Augsburg, Germany bInstitute for Machine Tools and Industrial Management, Technical University of Munich, Boltzmannstr. 15, 85748 Garching, Germany * Corresponding author. Tel.: +49-821-90678-143 ; fax: +49-821-90678-199. E-mail address: jonas.dhom@igcv.fraunhofer.de Abstract Higher energy densities and a high level of safety are decisive for the energy storage systems of electric vehicles to satisfy the high range requirements. The polymer-based solid-state battery is a promising technology that stands out due to its high energy density and theoretical safety advantages. Research into this innovative battery technology in Germany and Europe mainly focuses on the laboratory scale. For the production of polymer-based solid-state batteries, there is a lack of accessible information to transfer the laboratory scale to pilot production. Therefore, competence in upscaling the polymer-based composite cathode is essential. The current work investigates the cause-effect relationships of individual production processes of the polymer-based composite cathode in order to establish an in-depth understanding of how they can be scaled. In particular, mixing, coating, and calendering are compared with the corresponding processes of the conventional electrode production of lithium-ion battery cells to identify similarities and differences. The comparison demonstrates that the calendering process is particularly challenging due to the minimization of the porosity of the polymer-based composite cathode. For this reason, the challenges of calendering are discussed in detail. An existing empirical model of the calendering of lithium metal is used to enhance the comprehension of the calendering process. This results in a reduced material consumption and experimental setup time. By using a temporary foil to protect the calender rollers from the high adhesion, polymer-based composite cathodes can be calendered with a conventional roll-to-roll process. The results provide first informations of the scalability of the process of polymer-based composite cathodes from laboratory-based work to a larger scale. Â© 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 57th CIRP Conference on Manufacturing Systems 2024 (CMS 2024) 57th CIRP Conference on Manufacturing Systems 2024 (CMS 2024) 444 Jonas Dhom et al. / Procedia CIRP 130 (2024) 443â€“448 Nomenclature AM Active material CC Composite cathode CB Conductive carbon LCO Lithium cobalt oxide LFP Lithium iron phosphate LIB Lithium-ion battery LiTFSI Lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulphonyl)imide LMO Lithium manganese oxide NMC Lithium-nickel-manganese-cobalt oxide PEO Polyethylene oxide PVDF Poly(vinylidene difluoride) Ref. References SSB Solid-state battery SE Solid electrolyte 2. Fundamentals of solid-state batteries Depending on the solid electrolyte (SE), SSB can be clas- sified into organic and inorganic groups [7], as shown in Fig- ure 1. Combining both groups leads to the third class, the hy- brid SE [8]. The polymer-based SSB belongs to the organic SE, characterized by high flexibility and machinability in produc- tion [9]. In addition, the production costs are lower compared to other types of SSB; for example, sulfide solid-state batteries require precautionary safety measures in production due to the formation of hydrogen sulfide in contact with moisture, which increases production costs [8]. The polymer-based SE has ex- cellent contact with the electrodes, resulting in low interfacial resistance [9]. Due to the production aspects and low cost, the polymer-based SSB offers excellent potential for scaling up to a pilot scale. Organic solid electrolyte Inorganic solid electrolyte Oxide-based SSB SSB SSB SSB Polymer-based Hybrid-based Sulfide-based Fig. 1. Classification of the solid-state battery depending on the solid elec- trolyte. The oxide and sulfide SE belong to the inorganic group [10]. The oxide SE have high ionic conductivity and elasticity mod- ulus compared to the polymer-based SE, which is a good pre- requisite for resistance to lithium dendrites [11]. One challenge with high elastic modulus is to achieve sufficient surface con- tact with the electrodes [12]. The sulfidic SE is characterized by high ionic conductivity [13], but precautions must be taken dur- ing fabrication due to the strong reactivity of sulfides [14, 15]. Another disadvantage of sulfide SE is its poor stability against lithium metal anodes [16]. This publication centers on polymer- based SSB due to its advantageous features. The main functions of the SE are the same as the porous separa- tor of the LIB, which allow the transfer of lithium-ions between the two electrodes [6]. Comparing the cathode of LIB and the composite cathode of the SSB, the CC already contains the SE to create ionic pathways [13]. By using SE, it is possible to use a lithium metal anode, leading to a higher theoretical capacity [17, 18]. The fundamental steps of electrode production are similar in the production of LIB and SSB. Using a lithium metal anode, the processing must take place in a dry room or, better in an in- ert atmosphere to protect the moisture-sensitive anode from un- wanted reactions [19, 20]. A significant difference is that SSB has no electrolyte filling, as the SE is already present in the composite cathode and in the SE-layer. This eliminates a pro- cess step in SSB, which can reduce production costs. The objective is to significantly reduce the porosity of the CC to achieve high ionic conductivity, as each pore is an obsta- cle to lithium-ion transport [9, 21]. During the calendering step of the LIB electrodes and CC, different porosities are desired. Therefore, limited information can be transferred from the cal- endering of the LIB electrodes to the CC. One challenge is the high adhesion of the polymer-based CC, which must be consid- ered when applying a conventional calendering process to avoid damage of the two rollers. 3. Production polymer-based composite cathode Electrode production for the LIB and SSB is divided into mixing, coating & drying, and calendering. In the first step of wet processing the components active material (AM), conduc- tive carbon (CB), binder and solvent are mixed. In the case of CC, an SE is added to act as a binder and is responsible for ionic conductivity [13, 22, 23]. Lithium iron phosphate (LFP) is often used as an AM due to the low cost [23], as shown in table 1. All the formulations use PEO as polymer matrix, which is available in sufficient amounts. From the molar ratio of O(PEO)/Li(LiTFSI), it can be concluded that the formula- tions are very different, and no trends are yet recognizable. Er- abhoina and Thelakkat (2022) [24] demonstrated with polymer- based CC that an increase in the lithium salt leads to a decrease in crystallinity of PEO. This improves the ionic conductivity at low temperatures. This publication also showed that an increase in the amount of AM leads to a lower specific capacity of the CC. Table 1 illustrates that the formulations have different pro- portions of AM. From this, it can be concluded that it is not yet Jonas Dhom et al. / Procedia CIRP 130 (2024) 443â€“448 445 clear how large the AM content can be to achieve a sufficiently high ionic conductivity. For a CC with a high surface loading, ionic conductivity is particularly important [1]. In the wet coating process, viscosity is one of the most critical properties of rheology [25, 26]. The coating may become un- stable if the viscosity is too high for the coating shear rate. This results in an undulating coating or blocking of the coating tool. During coating, it must be determined which shear rate range occurs. For doctor blade coating, the shear rate is defined by the coating speed and the gap between the blade and the substrate foil [27]. In this process, the slurry is applied directly to the sub- strate foil respectively aluminum foil, which is equivalent to a conventional battery production process of LIB [13]. The coat- ing tool and the web speed can adjust the mass loading of the CC and the LIB electrodes. A drying step follows to remove the solvent from the electrodes. The solvent removal mainly causes porosity in the LIB electrodes and the CC [28, 29]. Table 1. Comparison of the composition of polymer-based composite cathodes Composite cathode Molar ratio of Mass ratio Ref. composition [wt%] O(PEO)/ of AM/SE Li(LiTFSI) LCO, PEO, LiTFSI, CB (75:11:4:10) 17.9:1 5.0:1 [30] LFP, PEO, LiTFSI, CB (50.2:26.9:17.8:5.1) 9.9:1 1.1:1 [23] NMC, PEO, PVDF, CB, LiTFSI (75.1:8.4:2.5:9.9:4) 13.7:1 5.0:1 [31] LFP, PEO, LiTFSI, CB (63:21.4:8.6:7) 16.2:1 2.1:1 [24] LFP, PEO, LiTFSI, CB (72.3:16.4:7.6:3.7) 14:1 3.0:1 [32] In the third step, the calendering, the CC is compacted to re- duce the porosity to a minimum, which is different from the LIB electrode. A side effect of calendering is increased adhesion between the coating and the substrate foil [13]. The polymer- based CC can not be calendered without adaptation due to the high adhesion in the calendering process, otherwise the rollers will be damaged. Based on the two main challenges of this pro- cess described above, the challenges are discussed in detail be- low. 4. Challenges in calendering of polymer-based composite cathodes Calendering describes the compaction of the electrodes, which allows for adjustment of the porosity. The CC is passed through a pair of rotating rollers that can be heated. The roller temperature can be set up to 150 Â°C [33, 34]. For electrodes, roller temperature adjustment results in lower spring-back. Furthermore, roller temperature adjustment requires a lower compaction pressure [35]. In the manufacturing of LIB elec- trodes, calendering has a significant effect on porosity and, thus on the electrochemical performance of the cell [22, 36]. The objective of compaction in LIB is to achieve the desired porosity [37]. For polymer-based CC, the objective is to reduce porosity to increase ionic conductivity, as each pore is an obstacle to lithium-ion transport, as shown in Figure 2 [21, 38]. Porosity describes the ratio of pore volume to total volume. With a high porosity, the available pathways are lower due to a high number of pores between the active material and the SE, which stops or divert the lithium-ions [21]. Subtrate foil Material of composite cathode Active material particle Lithium-ion Pores Solid electrolyte Fig. 2. Schematic representation of the structure of a composite cathode. The pores represent the free volume, and the interstices are filled with solid elec- trolyte [21]. Erabhoina and Thelakkat [24] showed with polymer-based CC that the calendering step can produce a pore-free and crack- free structure. This publication only showed that it is possible to produce a non-porous CC. However, the parameter setting and process expertise were not considered. In general, it should be noted that if the pressure is too high during calendering, it can lead to cracks in the foil or embrittlement of the material. [39]. Schilcher et al. [40] investigated the calendering behavior of cathode with the active material Lithium manganese oxide (LMO). In this observation, it was shown by too high mechan- ical stress that the LMO particle breaks. Due to the high stress in calendering, it must be ensured that after calendering, every particle of the active material is still in contact with the SE to have a lithium-ion pathway [32]. Compared to the LIB elec- trodes, the lithium-ions can only be transferred in a limited area between the particle of active material and the SE [41]. Helmers et al. [32] showed with polymer-based CC, the PEO 446 Jonas Dhom et al. / Procedia CIRP 130 (2024) 443â€“448 can be damaged at high shear stress during calendering. In the calendering of LIB, it has already been shown that electrode corrugation occurs at too high line pressures [42]. The cause is an inhomogeneous density distribution attributed to upstream process steps such as coating and drying. Due to the high adhesion of the polymer-based CC, the con- ventional roll-to-roll calendering process requires a foil to pro- tect the two rollers. Wurster [23] used a Mylar foil with a sili- cone coating on both sides for the calendering of the CC sheets. The calendering was carried out in several steps, in which the gap was reduced by 100 Âµm in each cycle. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis showed that silicone was probably added to the polymer-based CC by calendering with siliconized Mylar foil. Repeated calendering increased the silicone content on the surface of the CC. Another insight was that high pressures and tension in the calendering process lead to the decomposition of the LiTFSI on the surface of the polymer-based CC. The more frequently the calendering process was carried out, the more decomposition products could be detected. Wurster [23] con- cluded that the calendering process significantly influences the composition of the polymer-based surface of the CC. Currently, the polymer-based CC are compacted on a laboratory scale using uniaxial presses, or only sheets are calendered. In the calendering process, there is a lack of defined compaction steps for reducing porosity. The process requires expertise on which line load, roller temperature, and web speed can be used for calendering. A scalable process is presented below to con- sider the challenges and requirements in calendaring. 5. Calendering of polymer-based composite cathode To transfer the existing laboratory scale compaction process to pilot production, it is essential that the necessary process comprehension is available. In addition, the process must have low costs to compete with LIB production. One possibility is to use empirical modeling to gain detailed insights into the calen- dering of polymer-based CC. An empirical model already exists for the calendering of lithium-metal foils [43, 44]. This model has already been validated and describes a calendering process; therefore, it should be possible to transfer the model for calen- daring the polymer-based CC. To do this, the model must be adapted to the polymer-based CC to predict which porosity of the CC is achieved for different calendering process parameters. This new empirical model is presented below. The empirical model consists of three sections: input, model, and output, as shown in Figure 3. In the first step, the regression must be parameterized with experimental data of the polymer- based CC. The individual coefficients are determined using the weighted least squares method [45]. This makes it possible to predict the porosity of the polymer-based CC before calender- ing using input parameters such as line load and roller tempera- ture. Figure 3 shows an example of three-dimensional modeling with a constant web speed. Within the three-dimensional factor space, it should be possible to predict the porosity depending on the line load and the roller temperature. This model should aim to understand the cause-effect relationships in the calendering of polymer-based CC. In addition, the model should provide a better understanding of the reduction in porosity, which should reduce the experimental setup. As a result, the material con- sumption and the process adjustment can be reduced. Input Model Output Experimental Process web speed line load roller data parameters temperature Empirical modeling Porosity 1. Parameterization of the regression for empirical modeling 2. Input parameters for the prediction for the empirical modeling Fig. 3. Structure of the adapted empirical model of the calendering process for the polymer-based composite cathode [43]. To transfer the existing laboratory scale of compaction to a conventional roll-to-roll process for calendering, it is vital to protect the two rollers from the high adhesion of the polymer- based CC. One possibility is to use a temporary foil to protect the upper roll for a one-sided coated CC, as shown in Figure 4. Temporary foil Roller Active material particle Pores Substrate foil Solid electrolyte Fig. 4. Schematic illustration of the calendering of the polymer-based compos- ite cathode with temporary foil. In this process, the polymer-based CC is calendered together with the temporary foil. The temporary foil should have a high Youngâ€™s modulus to avoid affecting the calendering process. Additionally, the temporary foil should have a strong absorp- tion of the materials of the polymer-based CC to prevent the CC from sticking to the temporary foil after calendaring process due to the high adhesion. To design the process cost-effectively, the temporary foil needs to have low costs. For an environmen- tally friendly process, cleaning the foil after use must be pos- Jonas Dhom et al. / Procedia CIRP 130 (2024) 443â€“448 447 sible to reuse it. Therefore, the temporary foil must not be dis- posed after use. For a double-sided coated polymer-based CC, two temporary foils are required to protect the top and bottom rollers. The tem- porary foil can be made of polymers such as Mylar foil or metal such as copper foil. 6. Implementation on an industrial scale To gain in-depth insights into the cause-effect relationships of the calendering of polymer-based CC, the next step is to gen- erate experiments to adapt the empirical model. The line load, roller temperature, and web speed must be varied to parame- terize the model. This makes it possible to predict the porosity in the four-dimensional factor space. The model should be vali- dated with a confidence interval of 95% and with the coefficient of determination. The temporary foil used should not influence the empirical modeling, as the selected temporary foil should have a high Youngâ€™s modulus. Therefore, the model should be independent of the temporary foil. With the use of a suitable temporary foil, it must be considered that the radius and the weight of the coil is increased. The tem- porary foil can already be used after coating. This prevents the CC from sticking to the coil. The temporary foil must remain with the polymer-based CC until stacking or winding to prevent it from adhesion to tools and equipment. Due to the desired low porosity of the CC compared to conventional LIB electrodes, there is less flexibility during the winding or folding process [19]. Using a temporary foil, the polymer-based CC can be calen- dered on a conventional roll-to-roll process. This offers the possibility of transferring the compaction step from laboratory scale production to pilot scale production. 7. Conclusion The process steps for polymer-based CC outline that the significant challenges are in calendering. In this part of the process, the porosity must be significantly reduced to achieve high ionic conductivity. The high compression pressures in the calendering process lead to various challenges which are de- scribed in detail. To provide an in-depth insight into the cause- effect relationships in this process, an existing empirical model was adapted to the calendering of the polymer-based CC. This should reduce the adjustment of the process and reduce mate- rial consumption. The objective of this model is to provide first insights into the reduction of porosity of polymer-based CC in the calendering process. Thereby it should be proven which re- duction of porosity is possible. The polymer-based CC could be calendered using the conventional roll-to-roll process with a selected temporary foil. This makes it possible to scale the ex- isting laboratory to a pilot scale. The proposed adaptation of the calendering for CC fulfills the requirements and can be implemented on a pilot scale produc- tion. This enables further investigations of the calendering, e.g., the influence of surface loading on the reduction of porosity. Furthermore, the calendered polymer-based CC will be charac- terized electrometrically. This is done both at the CC level and at the cell level. For this purpose, cell tests will be carried out with different cell formats and configurations. Acknowledgements The presented work is part of the research project â€˜DiBatma-proâ€™ (Digitalisierung in der Batteriematerial-und Batteriezellenproduktion). We express our gratitude to the Fed- eral Ministry of Education and Research, the Bavarian Ministry of Economic Affairs, Regional Development and Energy, and the European Regional Development Fund for the project. Fur- thermore, we would like to thank our research partners for their constructive work and support with the DiBatma-pro project. Moreover, the authors are grateful for the support of Julian Wendel in the context of his student work and activities. References [1] F. He, W. Tang, X. Zhang, L. Deng, J. head.ce:title: Challenges and Opportunities in Composite Cathode Production of Polymer-Based Solid-State Batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fraunhofer Institute for Casting, Composite and Processing Technology IGCV, Am Technologiezentrum 10, 86159 Augsburg, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aFraunhofer Institute for Casting, Composite and Processing Technology IGCV, Am Technologiezentrum 10, 86159 Augsburg, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Machine Tools and Industrial Management, Technical University of Munich, Boltzmannstr. 15, 85748 Garching, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Machine Tools and Industrial Management

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bInstitute for Machine Tools and Industrial Management, Technical University of Munich, Boltzmannstr. 15, 85748 Garching, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Higher energy densities and a high level of safety are decisive for the energy storage systems of electric vehicles to satisfy the high range requirements. The polymer-based solid-state battery is a promising technology that stands out due to its high energy density and theoretical safety advantages. Research into this innovative battery technology in Germany and Europe mainly focuses on the laboratory scale. For the production of polymer-based solid-state batteries, there is a lack of accessible information to transfer the laboratory scale to pilot production. Therefore, competence in upscaling the polymer-based composite cathode is essential. The current work investigates the cause-effect relationships of individual production processes of the polymer-based composite cathode in order to establish an in-depth understanding of how they can be scaled. In particular, mixing, coating, and calendering are compared with the corresponding processes of the conventional electrode production of lithium-ion battery cells to identify similarities and differences. The comparison demonstrates that the calendering process is particularly challenging due to the minimization of the porosity of the polymer-based composite cathode. For this reason, the challenges of calendering are discussed in detail. An existing empirical model of the calendering of lithium metal is used to enhance the comprehension of the calendering process. This results in a reduced material consumption and experimental setup time. By using a temporary foil to protect the calender rollers from the high adhesion, polymer-based composite cathodes can be calendered with a conventional roll-to-roll process. The results provide first informations of the scalability of the process of polymer-based composite cathodes from laboratory-based work to a larger scale.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High energy density solid state lithium metal batteries enabled by sub-5 µm solid polymer electrolytes
Advanced materials (Deerfeld Beach, Fla.)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. He, W. Tang, X. Zhang, L. Deng, J. Luo, High energy density solid state lithium metal batteries enabled by sub-5 µm solid polymer electrolytes, Advanced materials (Deerfeld Beach, Fla.) 33 (45) (2021) e2105329. doi:10.1002/adma.202105329.

Nature Energy
